claims.claim_text: The December 2020 SolarWinds Orion supply-chain compromise reached government and private-sector organizations through malicious code inserted into trusted SolarWinds updates that were signed by SolarWinds.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, opening paragraph; section: Did You Know, bullet 1
claims.rationale: This is a real historical incident with named product, date, affected sectors, and delivery mechanism.

claims.claim_text: The KCSA curriculum explicitly includes Kubernetes Threat Model, Kubernetes Trust Boundaries and Data Flow, Supply Chain Security, Admission Control, and Threat Modelling Frameworks.
claims.span_hint: section: Why This Module Matters, final paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a named curriculum/exam-scope claim and the Linux Foundation exam page lists these domains directly.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes security is commonly framed with the 4C model: Cloud, Cluster, Container, and Code.
claims.span_hint: section: The 4C Model for Kubernetes Threats, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a named security framework claim and the official Kubernetes docs state the 4Cs explicitly.

claims.claim_text: STRIDE stands for Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information Disclosure, Denial of Service, and Elevation of Privilege.
claims.span_hint: section: STRIDE Applied to Kubernetes, opening paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a named threat-modeling framework with a canonical category expansion.

claims.claim_text: Provenance provides verifiable information about where an artifact came from and how it was produced, and that metadata should be evaluated against supply-chain policy.
claims.span_hint: section: Provenance, SBOMs, and Attestations, paragraphs 1 and 4
claims.rationale: The CNCF paper defines provenance and explains verification of provenance metadata against policy.

claims.claim_text: An SBOM is a detailed inventory of software components, libraries, and dependencies, and it helps consumers determine transitive dependencies and vulnerability exposure.
claims.span_hint: section: Provenance, SBOMs, and Attestations, paragraph 2; section: Did You Know, bullet 3
claims.rationale: This is a definitional claim about SBOMs and their security use.

claims.claim_text: Attestations are signed statements created during software-supply-chain steps, and verifiers should check them against supply-chain policy.
claims.span_hint: section: Provenance, SBOMs, and Attestations, paragraph 3
claims.rationale: This is a specific statement about attestation semantics and verification requirements.

claims.claim_text: Kubernetes admission webhooks receive admission requests, mutating webhooks run first, and validating webhooks can reject requests to enforce policy.
claims.span_hint: section: Policy Gates at the Cluster Boundary, opening paragraphs
claims.rationale: This is Kubernetes-specific admission behavior and the official docs describe the order and rejection semantics.

claims.claim_text: Specific products including Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper, Ratify, Connaisseur, and Sigstore-related components can implement parts of the policy-gate model.
claims.span_hint: section: Policy Gates at the Cluster Boundary, paragraph after policy-gate diagram
claims.anchor_text: Tools such as Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper, Ratify, Connaisseur, and Sigstore-related components can implement parts of this model.
claims.suggested_rewrite: Policy engines and artifact-verification components can implement parts of this model.
claims.rationale: The pedagogical point is that Kubernetes can rely on ecosystem policy tooling; the specific product list would require multiple tool-specific sources.

claims.claim_text: SLSA focuses on build integrity, provenance, and trust in the build platform as part of resisting tampering in the software supply chain.
claims.rationale: The claim is real and best supported by the SLSA specification, but `slsa.dev` is outside the current allowlist.

claims.claim_text: Admission webhook failure policy can be configured to ignore webhook errors/timeouts and allow the API request to continue, or to fail admission and reject the request.
claims.span_hint: section: Policy Gates at the Cluster Boundary, final paragraph
claims.rationale: This is a concrete Kubernetes webhook behavior that underpins the module's fail-open/fail-closed tradeoff claim.

claims.claim_text: Software-supply-chain metadata can be verified at the deployment gate, for example by a Kubernetes admission controller.
claims.span_hint: section: Provenance, SBOMs, and Attestations, final paragraph; section: Did You Know, bullet 4
claims.rationale: The CNCF paper explicitly names Kubernetes admission controllers as deployment-gate verifiers for supply-chain metadata.

further_reading.title: Cloud Native Security and Kubernetes
further_reading.why_relevant: Official Kubernetes guidance on trust boundaries, supply-chain controls, and runtime security layers.

further_reading.title: Dynamic Admission Control
further_reading.why_relevant: Authoritative reference for how Kubernetes admission webhooks evaluate and reject requests.

further_reading.title: CNCF Software Supply Chain Security Paper v2
further_reading.why_relevant: Deep background on provenance, SBOMs, attestations, policy verification, and deployment-gate design.

notes: Excluded diagrams, code blocks, quiz items, hands-on exercise steps, and non-factual teaching prose per schema rules. Repeated SolarWinds references were consolidated into one incident claim.
